title: What 'we'll give you a good rate' actually means

scripts.subtitle: Phrase-decode classic PAS
scripts.original_vo: Listen. When your bank tells you we'll give you a good rate, they mean good for them. Here's the math. 10am Tuesday. Real interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, is 7.22. Your bank quotes you 7.05. That looks fine. It's not. The gap is 2.4 percent. On a 10K wire that's 240 dollars gone. On 100K it's 2,400. Same percent. Scales with every wire you send. You never see it on a statement because it's baked into the rate itself. Banks compete on the word good. You should compete on the actual number. Stop. Open XE.com or just Google USD to CNY before you confirm the wire. If your quote is more than half a percent off, you're paying a silent margin. Period. WorldFirst quotes near the interbank rate so the number you see is the number you pay. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Okay, let us slow down and walk through what really happens when your bank says, we will give you a good rate. Picture it. You are sending money to your supplier in China. The bank is friendly. They say, do not worry, we will give you a good rate. That sounds caring. It is not. Good for who? Good for them. Here is what we mean. Every minute of every day, there is one real rate that banks use when they trade money with each other. People call it the interbank rate. Think of it as the wholesale price for money. On a normal Tuesday morning, the real wholesale price for one US dollar might be 7.22 Chinese yuan. Now, the rate your bank gives you on your wire? Maybe 7.05. Looks close. It is not. That little gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 10,000 dollar wire, that is 240 dollars quietly kept by your bank. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. Same percentage. Scales with every wire you send. And here is the part most people miss. You will never see this on your statement. There is no line that says, by the way, we kept this much. It is baked right into the rate. The fix is simple, and it is free. Before you click confirm on any wire, open a new tab. Type XE.com, or just Google USD to CNY. Compare what your bank quoted you with what the real rate is at that moment. If the gap is more than half a percent, your bank is keeping the difference. We built WorldFirst to fix this. Our rates sit close to that real wholesale number, so the rate you see is the rate you pay. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Word-for-word translation reveal
scripts.original_vo: Let me translate what your bank actually said. We'll give you a good rate. Translation. We'll give you a rate that sounds good next to the rate we'd give a tourist. 10am check. Interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, sitting at 7.22. Bank quote on your wire. 7.05. The word good is doing 2.4 percent of heavy lifting. On a 50K supplier payment that's 1,200 dollars your bank kept and never line-itemed. Stop accepting adjectives. Start asking for the number. Ask your bank, what's your USD-CNY rate today, then open Google and compare. Half a percent off is normal. Two percent off is them charging you for the word good. WorldFirst gives you near-interbank rates with no adjective games. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Let us translate what your bank actually said when they used the word good. We will give you a good rate. Sounds nice. Here is what it really means. We will give you a rate that sounds good when you put it next to the worst rate we could legally give a tourist at the airport. Not the real rate. The tourist rate. That is what good is being compared to. Let us prove it with real numbers. At 10am on a normal Tuesday, the real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate that banks use when they trade with each other, was 7.22. Your bank quoted you 7.05 for your wire. That tiny gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 50,000 dollar payment to your supplier, the bank quietly kept 1,200 dollars. There is no line on the statement that says, we kept this. It is hidden inside the rate itself. So here is what we want you to do. Next time your bank uses a word like good, just ask them for the number. Say, what is your exact USD to CNY rate today? Write it down. Then open Google and type USD to CNY. Compare the two. If your bank is half a percent off, that is normal. If they are two full percent off, they are charging you for the word good. We built WorldFirst to do this differently. Our rates sit close to the real wholesale number. No fancy words, just the number. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: We want to tell you about one word that quietly costs business owners more than almost any other word in banking. The word is good. As in, we will give you a good rate. Here is why that little word is so expensive. Picture Tuesday morning at 10am. The real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate banks use when they trade with each other, sits at 7.22. Your bank tells you they have a good rate for you. They quote you 7.05. That gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. On a 10,000 dollar wire, that is 240 dollars. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. Now think about this. Most importers do not just send one wire a year. They send wires every month. Sometimes every week. Now do the math over a whole year. The word good might have quietly cost you enough money to hire someone new. Or to build up your inventory before Q3. Or to drop your prices and beat a competitor. None of that ever shows up on a statement, because the cost is hidden inside the rate itself. Banks lean on the word good because most business owners are too busy to check. So here is the fix. It is free, and it takes 10 seconds. Open a new tab. Go to XE.com. Compare the rate. Or even simpler, Google USD to CNY. Then ask your bank for the actual number, not the adjective. We built WorldFirst so you can see the real rate before you click confirm. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Mechanism reveal, no accusation
scripts.original_vo: Here's what most business owners don't realize about the phrase good rate. Good is not a rate. It's a relative word. Good compared to what. Here's the answer your bank hopes you never ask. Tuesday 10am. Real interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, is 7.22. Your bank's good rate. 7.05. The gap is 2.4 percent. On every wire. Buried in the exchange rate itself, never a line item on the statement. So when the bank says good, they mean better than what they could legally charge a tourist. Not better than the real cost. Better than the worst case. The fix is one tab. XE.com. Google. Wise mid-market quote. All free. All real-time. Compare before you confirm. WorldFirst publishes rates near interbank so good actually means good. Search WorldFirst. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is something most business owners do not realize about the phrase good rate. Good is not really a rate. It is a word that compares one thing to another. So the moment you hear the word good, the question you should ask is, good compared to what? That is the question your bank really hopes you never ask out loud. Let us answer it for you. Picture Tuesday at 10am. The real wholesale rate for US dollars to Chinese yuan, the rate banks use when they trade between themselves, is 7.22. Your bank's good rate for your wire is 7.05. The gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. On every wire you send. And it is not a fee you can see. It is buried inside the exchange rate itself. So when your bank uses the word good, they mean better than the worst rate they could have given a tourist changing money at the airport. Not better than the real wholesale price. Better than the worst case. The fix is one browser tab. You have three free options. XE.com. A simple Google search for USD to CNY. Or check the Wise mid-market quote. All of them are free. All of them are updated in real time. Just compare before you click confirm. We built WorldFirst so the rate you see sits close to that real wholesale number. So when we say good, we actually mean good. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: If your relationship manager at the bank has ever told you, we will give you a good rate, this little test is going to save you real money. Here is how it works. Grab a piece of paper. Draw a line down the middle so you have two columns. In the left column, write down exactly what the bank quoted you. Let us say they offered 7.05 yuan for every US dollar on a wire to your supplier. In the right column, write what the real wholesale rate actually was at that exact minute. That is called the interbank rate, the rate banks use when they trade money with each other. Say it was 7.22. Now look at the gap between the two columns. That gap is 2.4 cents on every dollar. So on a 10,000 dollar wire, you quietly paid 240 dollars hidden in the rate. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is 1,200 dollars. On a 100,000 dollar wire, that is 2,400 dollars. None of it ever shows up as a fee, because it is built into the rate itself. The good news is you do not need a treasury team to catch this. You need one browser tab. Open XE.com, or just Google USD to CNY. Compare before you click confirm. If your bank is more than half a percent off the real number, that word good is doing all the work. We built WorldFirst so you see the live rate right before you confirm the wire. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is something for any business owner who sends money to a supplier in China. When your bank tells you, we will give you a good rate, we want you to understand exactly what that sentence is. It is marketing. It is not a number. Let us show you what good actually meant on a real wire last Tuesday. A friend of ours runs a small import business. He was sending 50,000 dollars to his supplier. The real wholesale rate that morning, the rate banks use when they trade with each other, was 7.22. His bank quoted him 7.05 and called it a good rate. That tiny gap quietly cost him 1,200 dollars. It was buried inside the rate itself. It did not appear as a fee on his statement. There was no line that said, by the way, we kept this much. Here is the simple idea we want you to take away. Words like good are not rates. Numbers are rates. So next time your bank uses an adjective, just ask them for the number. Then open Google and type USD to CNY. Compare them side by side. scripts.subtitle: I'll say it opinion-as-truth
scripts.original_vo: I'll say it. Good rate is the most successful piece of marketing the banks ever ran. Because good is comparative and they get to pick what it's compared to. You send a wire to your supplier in China. Bank says good rate. You feel taken care of. Meanwhile the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other, is sitting 2 to 3 percent better than what they just quoted you. On 50K, that's a thousand dollars or more, gone in one click. You'll never see it because the margin lives inside the rate, not on the statement. The fix is so simple. Google USD to CNY before you confirm. If you're more than half a percent off, you're paying for the word good. WorldFirst publishes rates near interbank. Numbers, not adjectives. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: We want to share an opinion. Good rate is probably the most successful piece of marketing that banks ever came up with. Here is why it works so well. The word good is a comparison word. It only means something when you put it next to something else. And the bank gets to quietly pick what to compare it to. So picture this. You are sending a wire to your supplier in China. Your bank says, we have a good rate for you today. You feel valued. You feel taken care of. Meanwhile, the real wholesale rate, the one banks use when they trade money with each other, is sitting 2 to 3 percent better than what they just quoted you. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is a thousand dollars or more, gone in one click. And you will never see it on your statement. Because the cost does not live in a fee. It lives inside the rate itself. The good news is the fix is simple. Before you click confirm on your next wire, open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then compare it to what your bank is offering you. If the gap is more than half a percent, you are paying for the word good. We built WorldFirst so the rate sits close to the real wholesale number. Numbers, not adjectives. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Stop accepting adjectives reframe
scripts.original_vo: Stop accepting adjectives from your bank. I'm serious. Good rate. Best rate. Preferred rate. Special rate. None of those are numbers. None of those tell you what your supplier actually receives in China. Here's what one looked like last week. Interbank USD-CNY, the rate banks use with each other, 7.22. Bank's good rate to a business owner I know, 7.05. Gap, 2.4 percent. On his 80K wire, that's almost 2K vanishing into the rate. You wouldn't sign a contract with the word good as a price. Don't accept it on a wire either. Ask for the number. Compare to Google. WorldFirst quotes near-interbank with the number shown before you confirm. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody tells / conspiratorial
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody is telling business owners about the phrase good rate. It's a trick of comparison. Banks compare your rate to a worse rate they could have given you and call it good. They don't compare it to the real interbank rate, the rate banks use with each other. On a typical day, that gap is 2 percent. Sometimes more. On 50K, that's 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. Never on the statement. The word good is doing all the work. Pull up Google. Search USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at your bank's quote. The gap is what good was hiding. WorldFirst quotes near interbank. Adjective free. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is something nobody is really telling business owners about the phrase good rate. It is a quiet trick of comparison. Let us explain. When a bank says good, they are comparing your rate to a worse rate they could have given you. That is their reference point. They are not comparing it to the real wholesale rate, which is the rate banks actually use when they trade money with each other. Two completely different reference points. On a normal day, the gap between what your bank quotes and the real wholesale rate is around 2 cents on every dollar. Sometimes more. On a 50,000 dollar wire, that is 1,000 to 1,500 dollars. Per wire. And it never shows up on your statement, because the cost is hidden inside the rate itself. The word good is doing all the heavy lifting. Here is how to catch it in 5 seconds. Open Google. Type USD to CNY. Look at the number. Then look at the rate your bank quoted you on the screen. The gap between those two numbers is what the word good was quietly hiding. We built WorldFirst so the rate sits close to the real wholesale number. Numbers, not adjectives. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Personal energy + verify-it-yourself
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for any business owner who has ever heard their bank say we'll give you a good rate. I see this every week. The relationship manager calls. They use the word good. The business owner feels valued. The wire goes out. The supplier gets less than expected. And nobody connects the dots. The dots are this. Good is not a rate. It's a feeling. The actual rate, the real interbank rate that banks use with each other, you can pull up on Google in 5 seconds. Search USD to CNY. Compare. If your bank is 2 percent off, the word good cost you 2 percent of every wire you sent this year. That's real money. Stop trusting the adjective. Trust the number. WorldFirst gives you the number. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: This one is for any business owner who has ever picked up a phone and heard their bank say, we will give you a good rate. We see the same story play out every week. Picture it. The relationship manager calls. They sound friendly. They use the word good. The business owner feels valued, feels like they are being looked after. The wire goes out. A few days later, the supplier in China receives a little less than expected. And nobody ever joins the dots between the two. Let us join them for you. Good is not a rate. Good is a feeling. The actual rate, the real wholesale rate that banks use when they trade money between themselves, you can find in 5 seconds on Google. Just type USD to CNY. The number is right there. Compare it to what your bank just quoted you. If your bank is 2 cents off on every dollar, then the word good has been quietly costing you 2 percent on every wire you sent all year. That is real money. Real margin. So next time, trust the number, not the adjective. We built WorldFirst to give you that number, right on the screen. Have a look. Just search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: Core teaching (the word 'good' is unverifiable until compared to interbank) is genuinely useful and defensible. The specific 7.22 vs 7.05 example is presented as if it's a single observed moment, which is fair as illustration. Main red flag is the universal-feeling 2.4 percent presented as a typical case, when actual spreads vary by bank, currency pair, wire size, and tier. 'Number you see is the number you pay' on WF side slightly overstates because CN-side receiving fees can still apply. The 'half a percent off' threshold is a reasonable heuristic but is presented as a hard rule.

skeptic_comments.text: 7.22 vs 7.05? One snapshot only what. CNY rate move every minute lah.
Half a percent threshold, who set this rule? Sound made up.
My Maybank quote not so bad. Maybe US bank different.
Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not?
Sound like ad. Show me real screenshot side by side la.

PH2.analysis: The translation framing (good rate = good compared to tourist rate) is clever but slightly snarky and could put a banking-defensive viewer on edge. The 7.22 vs 7.05 single-moment example is consistent with PH1. 'No adjective games' on WF side is a fair brand promise but reads as marketing. 'Half a percent normal, two percent is them charging you' is again a heuristic presented as if it's universal. The core mechanism teaching is sound.

skeptic_comments.text: Tourist rate? Cmon now, business wire not same as money changer lah.
1,200 on 50K, ok the math works. But once-off example or always?
Half a percent normal, two percent bad. Where got rule like this?
WorldFirst no adjective game, sure or not? Read your own marketing first.
What's your fee model then? Free? Hidden somewhere also?

PH3.analysis: The compounding consequences (cost you a hire, your Q3 inventory buffer, your competing margin) are dramatic extrapolations from a single 2.4 percent figure. Most SMBs will see this as stretched. 'Banks lean on the word good because business owners don't check' is slightly accusatory of the audience. The 7.22 vs 7.05 example is fine, but stacking it into 'a year of supplier wires' without specifying volume is the same compounding risk as PH2 in the reference set. Most defensible part is the verify-yourself instruction.

skeptic_comments.text: Cost me a hire? Bro you don't know my volume. Maybe I do 2 wires a year.
Business owners don't check, ok so you call us lazy? Thanks.
Where 2.4% come from consistently? My bank not so bad.
Q3 inventory buffer? Sound like consultant talk. Just show real rate la.
Who is WorldFirst? Need license check first.

PH4.analysis: Cleanest of the Hormozi set. The 'good compared to what' question is genuinely useful pedagogy. The 7.22 vs 7.05 example is bounded as illustration not universal claim. Spelling out interbank ('the rate banks use with each other') and listing free verification tools (XE.com, Google, Wise mid-market) is concrete and actionable. 'Better than what they could charge a tourist' is mild snark but defensible because banks do tier rates. Main red flag is still that 2.4 percent reads as typical when it's just one example.

skeptic_comments.text: Good compared to what, ya this question actually useful.
XE Google Wise, ok concrete. I can verify. Fair enough.
Tourist rate comparison, my BNM regulated bank not the same lah.
Still want to know who WorldFirst is. License? Parent company?
2.4% on this one example. What about other days when bank rate is closer?

PH5.analysis: The two-column test is the cleanest teaching device in the Hormozi set. Concrete amounts at 10K, 50K, 100K let the viewer find their own size. 'One browser tab' is honest minimum-friction framing. 'Half a percent off' threshold is again a heuristic but here it's labeled as rule of thumb, not law. 'The word good is doing the work' is softer than 'doing the lying' (which was the original) and lands without insulting bank or viewer. Most defensible script in the Hormozi set.

skeptic_comments.text: Two column test ok I can do this. Practical advice for once.
Half percent threshold still feel made up but at least sound reasonable.
Live rate shown before confirmation, sure WorldFirst do this?
Who is WorldFirst? Never hear before in KL.
Compare before confirm, ok useful tip even if I don't switch.

PG1.analysis: 'Adjectives are not rates' is clean Gary Vee opinion landing. The single 7.22 vs 7.05 example with the friend story personalizes it. 'No line item' is technically accurate and a legitimate teaching point. Main risk is the friend anecdote is unverifiable, and 'good cost him 1,200 dollars' is one example presented to imply norm. WF claim 'actual number shown' is a brand promise that a skeptic will want to test.

skeptic_comments.text: Your friend, sure or just a made up example?
Adjective not rate, fine. But all banks use marketing words lah, normal.
1,200 on 50K once, but my bank maybe charge 500. Different.
Who is WorldFirst? Need to research first.
Sound like ad. Got real customer testimonial from SE Asia SME?

PG2.analysis: 'Most successful piece of marketing the banks ever ran' is opinion framed as opinion, defensible Gary Vee voice. The '2 to 3 percent' range is more honest than a single 2.4 percent figure and shows it varies. 'Margin lives inside the rate, not on the statement' is accurate. 'Half a percent off' threshold again presented without source. WF claim 'numbers not adjectives' is clean brand positioning that doesn't overstate function.

skeptic_comments.text: Most successful marketing, fair opinion. Bit dramatic but ok.
2 to 3 percent range, better than fixed number. But still got source?
Half percent threshold rule, where from? Sound arbitrary.
Margin in rate not statement, ya this part true actually.
Who is WorldFirst? Got license in Malaysia or just online thing?

PG3.analysis: 'Selling you adjectives' as opener is clean reframe and lands the phrase-decode angle. Listing 'good rate, best rate, preferred rate, special rate' is genuine and useful pattern recognition. The 80K example differs from prior 50K which adds variety but the 2.4 percent is again consistent across scripts which a binge-watcher could question. Wouldn't sign a contract with adjective as price is a strong analogy that a skeptic might accept.

skeptic_comments.text: Best rate preferred rate special rate, ya banks do use these. Pattern is real.
80K wire example, who you know that sends 80K? Big SME already.
Contract with adjective as price analogy, ok this one land.
2.4% again, every script same number. You only got one data point?
Show me WorldFirst's actual rate vs my Maybank. Side by side.

PG4.analysis: 'Trick of comparison' is sharp framing. The teaching that banks compare to worse-internal-rate not real interbank is genuinely useful and largely accurate. '2 percent typical, sometimes more' is honest range. '1,000 to 1,500 dollars per wire' on 50K aligns with the 2-3 percent range, internally consistent. 'Never on the statement' is technically accurate. Main risk is the 'trick' framing is mildly accusatory and a skeptic may push back.

skeptic_comments.text: Trick of comparison, ok this actually a good way to frame it.
2% typical, source please? Or your opinion only?
1000 to 1500 on 50K. Math checks out. But every bank like this?
Never on statement, ya FX margin baked in rate. This part true.
Who is WorldFirst? Need license proof before I move money.

PG5.analysis: Most defensible script in the Gary Vee set. The RM-calls-you scenario is recognizable to any importer. 'Good is not a rate, it's a feeling' is clean opinion landing that doesn't claim a number. The 'every week' framing is softened to 'this happens' which removes the I-see-this overreach. Verify-yourself instruction (Google USD to CNY, 5 seconds) is concrete and free. No specific dollar amount claimed, only a 2 percent illustration. Light on numbers means light on red flag risk.

skeptic_comments.text: RM call use word good, ya this part very real. Happens to me also.
Good is a feeling not a rate, ok decent line.
2 percent of every wire this year, big assumption on volume.
Google 5 seconds, fair enough. I can do this myself.
Who is WorldFirst? Got branch in Malaysia or only app?
